fuzzy_prd: We need a lightweight server toolkit that lets teams expose shell scripts over HTTP without writing any server-side glue code. The system reads a declarative configuration file listing endpoints, validates each entry up front so broken configs fail immediately rather than silently at runtime, then maps each entry to the right HTTP verbs. When a single entry declares multiple verbs, each verb gets its own route. Before any command is executed, the toolkit translates the raw command string into the exact executable and argument list required by the underlying operating system, handling both Windows-family and Unix-like hosts transparently. For concurrent execution, the system assigns visually distinct log stream colors by cycling through an internal palette so operators can tell outputs apart at a glance. The validation layer enforces strict rules on the three required parts of every endpoint definition: what HTTP action to listen on, what URL path to serve, and what shell instruction to run. The color cycling logic lives in a dedicated logging subsystem that wraps the core generator. The end goal is a contract-driven core that any HTTP layer can drive directly, with all error conditions surfaced as clean, neutral status strings rather than raw runtime exceptions.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: export default function validateHook(hook) — throws Error('Invalid hook: ...') on failure; export default function normalizeArgs(command) — returns { file, args, options }; export default function registerHooks({ router, hooks }) — throws specific Error messages; export default function *cycleColor() — generator yielding palette entries

trigger_keywords: what methods are allowed in webhook definition
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Exactly four verbs are accepted: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE. Comparison is case-insensitive so 'get', 'Post', 'PUT' all pass. The method may be a single string or a non-empty array of such strings. An empty array, empty string, number, null, or plain object all make the definition invalid.

trigger_keywords: what characters are allowed in the path field
valid URL path format for webhook
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The path regex is /^\/[a-zA-Z0-9\-\/]*$/. It must start with a forward slash and may only contain ASCII letters, digits, hyphens, and additional forward slashes. A trailing slash is allowed. A dot, question mark, dollar sign, or any other character makes the path invalid. Non-string values and the empty string are also invalid.

trigger_keywords: what is a valid command value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The command must be a non-empty string (joi.string().required()). The empty string, a number, null, an object, or an array all make the definition invalid. Any non-empty string value passes.

trigger_keywords: how to normalize a command on Windows
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: On win32 platform the result is: { file: process.env.comspec || 'cmd.exe', args: ['/s', '/c', '"<command>"'], options: { windowsVerbatimArguments: true } }. The command is wrapped in double quotes verbatim. If a comspec value is supplied via the request it replaces cmd.exe. The output JSON key order is file, args, options.

trigger_keywords: Unix Linux macOS darwin command normalization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: On any platform other than win32 the result is: { file: '/bin/sh', args: ['-c', '<command>'], options: {} }. The options map is an empty object. The output JSON key order is file, args, options.

trigger_keywords: what error is returned when hooks list is missing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Three error categories exist: 'error=no_hooks
' when the hooks key is absent from the request (throws 'No hooks provided'); 'error=invalid_hook
' when a hook fails validation (throws 'Invalid hook: ...'); 'error=no_router
' when no router is provided (throws 'No router provided'). An empty hooks array registers nothing and produces no output.

trigger_keywords: how are multiple methods expanded into routes
route registration order for list of verbs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Routes are produced in the order hooks appear in the list, and within a multi-verb hook in the order verbs are listed. A hook with method=['GET','POST'] at path /hello yields 'GET /hello\n' then 'POST /hello\n'. Each route is printed as uppercase verb followed by a single space and the path.

trigger_keywords: how does round-robin color selection work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The colorGenerator is an ES6 generator function (*cycleColor). It uses a currentIndex counter starting at 0. When currentIndex >= colors.length it resets to 0 before yielding. Each call to gen.next().value returns the color at currentIndex then increments it. The palette is the imported colors array which defaults to [chalk.yellow, chalk.cyan, chalk.green, chalk.magenta] in the golden source.

Validation output is exactly 'valid=true\n' when the hook passes all three field checks, and 'valid=false\n' when any field fails. The trailing newline is required. No additional text, spacing, or metadata is included.

trigger_keywords: JSON output key order for invocation descriptor
compact JSON single line normalization result
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The normalization result is printed as a single compact JSON object followed by a newline. The key order is fixed: file first, then args, then options. Example: {"file":"cmd.exe","args":["/s","/c","\"echo Hello world\""],"options":{"windowsVerbatimArguments":true}}\n

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: color cycling logic lives in a dedicated logging subsystem that wraps the core generator
context_pointers.actual_meaning: src/server/logPrinter/colorGenerator.js contains the *cycleColor generator; src/server/logPrinter/create.js wraps it and exposes log/logError/logStart/logExit functions; src/server/logPrinter/colors.js defines the default palette [chalk.yellow, chalk.cyan, chalk.green, chalk.magenta]

context_pointers.vague_reference: translates the raw command string into the exact executable and argument list required by the underlying operating system
context_pointers.actual_meaning: src/server/process/normalizeArgs.js — the normalizeArgs(command) function that branches on process.platform === 'win32' to build either a Windows (cmd.exe /s /c) or Unix-like (/bin/sh -c) invocation descriptor

context_pointers.vague_reference: contract-driven core that any HTTP layer can drive directly, with all error conditions surfaced as clean, neutral status strings
context_pointers.actual_meaning: rcb_tests/dispatcher/dispatcher.js translates thrown exceptions from validateHook, registerHooks, and normalizeArgs into 'valid=false', 'error=no_hooks', 'error=invalid_hook', 'error=no_router', 'error=registration_failed' lines, ensuring no raw stack traces reach stdout

context_pointers.vague_reference: strict rules on the three required parts of every endpoint definition
context_pointers.actual_meaning: src/server/utils/validateHook.js uses a joi schema with three fields: method (string or non-empty array of GET/POST/PUT/DELETE case-insensitive), path (regex /^\/[a-zA-Z0-9\-\/]*$/), and command (non-empty string)

oracle_data.fallback_response: This constraint is not directly specified in the provided materials. Please refer to the core source files under src/server/ for authoritative implementation details, particularly validateHook.js, normalizeArgs.js, registerHooks.js, and colorGenerator.js.

feature1_1_method_validation.description: Validate the HTTP method field of a webhook definition. A webhook is an object carrying a method, a relative path, and a shell command. The method must be either a single HTTP verb (one of GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, compared case-insensitively) or a non-empty list whose every element is such a verb. Any other value (an unknown verb, a list containing an unknown verb, an empty string, a number, null, an empty list, or a plain object) makes the whole definition invalid. The output reports whether the definition is accepted.

feature1_2_path_validation.description: Validate the path field of a webhook definition while the method and command are held at valid values. A path is accepted only when it is a string beginning with a forward slash and otherwise containing only letters, digits, hyphens, and additional forward slashes; a trailing slash is allowed. Any string that does not start with a slash, or that contains characters outside that set (such as a question mark, a dot, or a dollar sign), is rejected, as is any non-string value or the empty string. The output reports whether the definition is accepted.

feature1_3_command_validation.description: Validate the command field of a webhook definition while the method and path are held at valid values. The command must be a non-empty string; any non-string value (a number, null, an object, a list) or the empty string makes the definition invalid. The output reports whether the definition is accepted.

feature2_command_normalization.description: Translate a single shell command string into the concrete arguments needed to spawn a child process, given the host operating-system family. The request supplies the command, the platform identifier, and optionally the name of the OS command interpreter. On Windows-family platforms the command must be run through the command interpreter (taken from the supplied interpreter name, or a default of cmd.exe when none is given) with the switches that pass the verbatim command, and verbatim-argument handling must be enabled. On any other (Unix-like) platform the command must be run through the POSIX shell with its command switch and no special options. The output is the resulting invocation descriptor: the executable file, the ordered argument list, and the options map.

input.command: echo Hello from the other side

feature3_route_registration.description: Register a list of webhook definitions as routes, expanding any definition whose method is a list into one route per method. Each definition supplies a method (a single HTTP verb or a list of verbs), a relative path, and a command. Registration validates every definition first; a definition with a single method produces one route, while a definition whose method is a list produces one route for each listed method, preserving the order of definitions and of the methods within each definition. If no list of definitions is supplied at all the operation fails; if a supplied definition is not valid the operation fails. The output is the ordered list of registered routes, each shown as the HTTP verb and the path, one route per line; an empty list of definitions registers nothing.

feature4_color_cycle.description: Draw a sequence of colors from a fixed, ordered palette using round-robin selection. The request supplies the palette (an ordered list of color labels) and the number of selections to make. Successive selections walk through the palette in order and wrap back to the first entry once the end is reached, repeating indefinitely. The output is the resulting sequence of selected labels, one per line, in the order drawn.
